perturbation.original_text: 1.7 shall not, and shall procure that any associated company of yours shall not, employ, seek to employ, or solicit any pe rson employed by us or the Business or who holds or otherwise has access to Confid ential Information either at the date of this Agreement or at any time during negotiations for the sale of the Business or during the period of twelve months from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.7 shall not solicit any person employed by us during active negotiations.
perturbation.explanation: By severely limiting the non-solicitation clause to only 'active negotiations' and removing restrictions on employing or seeking to employ, and also removing the 12-month restriction after the agreement and restrictions on associated companies, this significantly weakens the protection for Mergers & Acquisitions. This contradicts the broad confidentiality undertaking outlined in Section 1.1, which aims to protect the Business and its information, potentially allowing the Recipient to indirectly benefit from Confidential Information gained during negotiations through future hiring. This change in 1.7 makes section 1.1 contradictory and creating uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1 shall keep secret and confidential our discussions and any information about the Business supplied to you or your advisers by us or by our advisers, whether before or after this Agreement and any analyses or other documents which may be made or prepared by you which contai n or otherwise reflect such information or your review of or interest in the Business (“Confidential Information”), and not disclose to any third party any Co nfidential Information (except such information which comes into the public domain other than as a result of the disclosure by you or by your employees, agents or advisers who need to know such information for the purpose of eval uating whether or not, and on what terms, you might proceed with the proposed purchase);
perturbation.justification: The original text of 1.7 included a broad non-solicitation clause that extended to associated companies, restricted employment and solicitation, and covered a 12-month period after the agreement. The changed text significantly narrows this clause to only solicitations during active negotiations. This weakened 1.7 now contradicts section 1.1, which requires keeping confidential any information about the business, as the recipient could now potentially benefit from confidential information through future hiring practices, something the original 1.7 aimed to prevent.

perturbation.original_text: 2.5 This agreement shall expire two years from the date of execution.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific expiration timeframe of 'two years from the date of execution' creates an indefinite agreement, but then it is followed by other clauses that suggest the agreement has a limited timeline. This indefinite duration introduces potential conflicts with other clauses that assume a finite engagement, making enforcement unpredictable. If, for example, confidential information is revealed five years after the agreement, it becomes unclear whether the confidentiality obligations still apply, creating an in-text contradiction with the rest of the agreement, and thus creating uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.4 the provisions of this Agreement shall continue in effect notwithstanding any decision not to proceed with any possible transaction relating to the Business or any return or destruction of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text specified a two-year expiration, providing a definitive timeframe for the agreement. The changed text removes this timeframe, making the agreement indefinite. The contradicted text states that the agreement's provisions continue even after a decision not to proceed with a transaction, which implicitly assumes a finite agreement. Removing the original expiry term introduces uncertainty as to how long the 'provisions' in contradicted text will last for since the original text's finite time frame has been removed, thus a strong contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 1.  UNDERTAKING In consideration of our Agreement to supply to you certain written or oral information relating to the Business and entering into discussions relating to the sale of the Business to you (howsoever structured), you the Recipient hereby undertake to us, the Vendor for our benefit and that of the Busine ss, except to the extent that we have given prior written consent, whether or not negoti ations for the sale of the Business proceed, that you:
perturbation.changed_text: 1. UNDERTAKING You the Recipient hereby undertake to us, the Vendor for our benefit and that of the Business, that you:
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'In consideration of our Agreement to supply to you certain written or oral information relating to the Business and entering into discussions relating to the sale of the Business to you (howsoever structured), except to the extent that we have given prior written consent, whether or not negotiations for the sale of the Business proceed' the agreement lacks clearly defined consideration from the vendor, meaning that the specific obligation from the vendor that creates the deal and makes the receipient obligated to the vendor. Consideration is a fundamental aspect of contract law. If the vendor does not provide the consideration promised, then the Recipient is under no obligation. This creates an in-text contradiction as it nullifies the entire agreement, creating uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1 shall keep secret and confidential our discussions and any information about the Business supplied to you or your advisers by us or by our advisers, whether before or after this Agreement and any analyses or other documents which may be made or prepared by you which contai n or otherwise reflect such information or your review of or interest in the Business (“Confidential Information”), and not disclose to any third party any Co nfidential Information (except such information which comes into the public domain other than as a result of the disclosure by you or by your employees, agents or advisers who need to know such information for the purpose of eval uating whether or not, and on what terms, you might proceed with the proposed purchase);
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the Recipient's undertaking is 'In consideration of our Agreement to supply to you certain written or oral information'. The changed text removes this consideration, which according to the explanation creates a situation where the vendor's obligations are unclear, potentially nullifying the entire agreement. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which outlines the Recipient's obligation to keep information confidential. If there is no clear consideration, then the confidential obligation is nullified. Therefore, there is a strong in-text contradiction because without clear consideration, the obligations in 1.1 become uncertain and potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 1.3.1 shall, in connection with the possible sale of the Business, make contact only with Mergers & Acquisitions and not with any other person or persons including our officers, empl oyees or advisers unless otherwise instructed; and 1.3.2 shall not discuss with any of our su ppliers, customers or sub-contractors, or with any of our officers, employees , agents or advisers, your possible interest in the Business or any as pects of its business or trading relationships;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3.1 shall, in connection with the possible sale of the Business, make contact with Mergers & Acquisitions and not with any other person or persons including our officers, empl oyees or advisers unless otherwise instructed;
perturbation.explanation: By removing the prohibition on discussing with suppliers, customers, or subcontractors the recipient's interest in the business, the contract creates a loophole allowing for potential leaks and disruption of the Business's operations. This change contradicts the broader confidentiality undertaking in Section 1.1, which seeks to prevent disclosure of Confidential Information. The ability to discuss the sale with key stakeholders (suppliers, customers) introduces risks that the overall purpose of confidentiality is undermined, creating an in-text contradiction, creating uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1 shall keep secret and confidential our discussions and any information about the Business supplied to you or your advisers by us or by our advisers, whether before or after this Agreement and any analyses or other documents which may be made or prepared by you which contai n or otherwise reflect such information or your review of or interest in the Business (“Confidential Information”), and not disclose to any third party any Co nfidential Information (except such information which comes into the public domain other than as a result of the disclosure by you or by your employees, agents or advisers who need to know such information for the purpose of eval uating whether or not, and on what terms, you might proceed with the proposed purchase);
perturbation.justification: The original text prohibited discussing the recipient's interest with suppliers, customers, or subcontractors. The changed text removes this prohibition. Section 1.1 requires keeping discussions and information about the business confidential. By removing the prohibition, the changed text now allows discussions that could reveal confidential information, directly contradicting the confidentiality requirement in Section 1.1. This aligns with the provided explanation and the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2 you agree that our rights under this Ag reement may be assigned in whole or in part to any purchaser of the Business, which may enforce this Agreement to the same extent and in the same manner as we can enforce it;
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2 you agree that our rights under this Agreement may be assigned to any purchaser of the Business;
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'in whole or in part' and 'which may enforce this Agreement to the same extent and in the same manner as we can enforce it', the scope of the assignment is left ambiguous. Can only the entire agreement be assigned or parts of it? And most critically, without stating the purchaser may enforce the agreement in the same manner, it is unclear if the purchaser even has the right to enforce the agreement which creates a huge contradiction to the whole purpose of 2.2 as is meant to give that right, creating uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.2 you agree that our rights under this Agreement may be assigned to any purchaser of the Business;
perturbation.justification: The original text clearly states that the rights can be assigned 'in whole or in part' and that the purchaser can enforce the agreement 'to the same extent and in the same manner'. The changed text removes this clarity, creating ambiguity about the scope of assignment and the purchaser's enforcement rights. This removal contradicts the implied understanding of the original clause's purpose and creates uncertainty about whether the purchaser even has the right to enforce the agreement, making the contradicted text logically incompatible and therefore creating a strong in-text contradiction. The perturbation type is 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction', which is in alignment with the changed text.
